title: Mausūkhi wa Sifāh: Adjectives and Descriptions in Classical Arabic
summary: Session focuses on the grammatical structure of adjectives (sifah) and their described nouns (mausukh) in Modern Standard Arabic. The teacher emphasizes that adjectives follow the noun they describe, unlike English. Key examples include 'Imratan ḥamḍān' (a good woman) and terms from religious texts like 'Sūrat al-Ḍuha'. Students practice constructing sentences with proper case endings (ḥarakāt) and gender agreement.
key_points: Adjectives in Arabic follow the noun they describe (e.g., امرأة حسنة — imra’atun ḥusn𝑎n).
Both the noun (mausūkh) and adjective (sifah) must agree in gender, number, and case.
'Sifah' comes after 'mausūkh', as seen in Quranic examples like 'ṣalātunal-ghadawī' (morning prayer).
Case endings (ḍammah, kasrah, fathah) on adjectives must match the preceding noun’s state.
Practice sentences included describing people and objects with proper agreement: e.g., ‘al-dukturatu al-jādītatu’ (the serious female doctor).
questions: [00:23:56] Q: How does 'mausūkh' relate to 'sifah'? — A: The adjective describes the noun, which is its direct object grammatically.
